title: First Night
html:
The eastern quarter is 20 minutes from the platform street on foot, through 3 road widths of city that get progressively less interested in presenting themselves well.

The first tier, closest to the platform, is where the money has settled. The buildings are finished, the storefronts active, and the people moving through it have the particular ease of anyone who knows exactly where they're going. They watch the column of 61 the way you'd watch a delivery cart cut across foot traffic: a logistics problem, briefly, and then not their problem anymore.

The second tier narrows. The road surface changes from fitted stone to packed earth and the buildings get their upper floors added in wood rather than stone, the additions sitting at odd angles to the original structures. A market is running on a side street to the left: open stalls, no fixed prices from the look of the negotiation happening at the nearest one. A woman behind a stall loaded with dried goods watches the column pass without expression. She's been watching new arrivals file past this corner for 6 cycles. Whatever she thinks of us isn't on her face.

The third tier is where Caitlin stops.

The building she takes us to is a workshop at street level with 2 floors above it, the kind of structure that started as one thing and got modified by whoever needed it to be something else. A man is visible through the workshop's open front, working at a bench in the interior. He looks up when Caitlin stops at the door, reads the column of people behind her, and goes back to the bench.

"Bram," Caitlin says. "We need the upper two."

"Cycle 7?" he asks without looking up.

"61 of them."

He sets down whatever he's working on, which turns out to be a blade component of some kind, and wipes his hands on a cloth at his hip. He's a big man, somewhere north of 40, with the kind of forearms that come from actual work rather than a gym. Cycle 2 or 3, from the wear in his face and clothing.

"8 in the upper floor," he says. "14 in the second if they sleep on the boards. Anything more than that is your problem, Cait."

That's 22 out of 61, which means the other 39 need to find somewhere else before the light goes. The arithmetic is its own kind of pressure.

"I'll take the 8 spots," Se-ah says, from beside me. "Core group. The rest of you need to establish contact with other buildings in this tier tonight." She says it to the column at a volume that carries. "Pairs at minimum. Do not go alone. Do not go toward the market district after dark. Come back to this building at first light."

A few people in the column look at me. I look at Se-ah.

She's not wrong about any of it. I don't say anything.

The Covenant finds the column on the road outside Bram's workshop 10 minutes after we arrive, which means they've been tracking us from the platform street the whole time.

There are 4 of them, in pale grey with a circular emblem on the chest that I don't get a close look at. The one leading them is a man in his mid-forties, well-fed in the way that comes from regular access to food rather than body type, and he moves through the column's stragglers with the practiced smoothness of someone who's run this approach before. Which, by Cycle 7, he has.

Rourke stops at a position that puts him visible to the largest number of people at once. He says they have had a hard day, that the Covenant remembers what the tutorial takes out of you. His voice carries the warmth of a prepared speech.

2 of the grey-coated people behind him are carrying flat-bottomed baskets. The smell coming off them is bread, still warm. Real bread, the kind made from something that required a grain supply and a working oven, which in Eoran means access to resources most of the 61 don't have yet.

About a third of the column is looking at the baskets.

"My name is Elder Rourke," the man continues. "The Covenant has housed and fed every new arrival since Cycle 3. We ask for nothing tonight. Eat, rest, and we will speak again in the morning when you've had time to think clearly."

He doesn't look at me. He doesn't look at Se-ah. He's reading who in the crowd is still shaky, who hasn't eaten since before the tutorial, who's been holding themselves together on tension that's starting to run out now that the gate is closed and the fight is over.

The eagle country man steps up beside me. He's been quiet since the platform, keeping pace, watching the city with the specific attention of someone building a map in their head. He's big across the shoulder and the way he moves says he's used to accounting for it in tight spaces. He leans toward me and keeps his voice low.

"He's going to take half of them," he says.

"Probably," I agree.

"You're not going to stop it?"

"They're adults. The Covenant houses and feeds people. The terms are bad but they're not dangerous, from what Caitlin said."

"30% of everything and mandatory attendance."

"Versus sleeping on the street in a city that's been running for 6 cycles without us in it." I watch Rourke hand the first piece of bread to a woman near the front of the column. She takes it without meeting his eyes. "There are worse deals on the board."

He's quiet for a moment. "Marcus Webb," he says.

"Eric."

"I know." He tips his chin toward the column. "The woman beside me is Chen Liling. She was doing her graduate exchange year in sakura country when this started. She's 23."

I look. The panda country woman from the platform is standing 2 meters back, watching Rourke with an expression that's reading the performance rather than the bread. She notices me looking and doesn't look away.

Marcus keeps watching the Covenant distribution. "I was a firefighter. Eagle country, midwest. 11 years."

"That explains the staircase pace," I say.

He almost smiles. "What were you?"

"Restaurant worker."

He looks at the machete still in my hand. He doesn't ask the question that goes with that look.

Rourke takes 23 people. He does it without pressure, offering the bread first, letting the smell do the work, and then presenting the offer in the kind of plain language that sounds reasonable when you're tired and have nowhere obvious to sleep. Housing in the northern residential tier, meals included, terms to be discussed formally in the morning. He's careful not to say 30% until the morning. He's careful about a lot of things.

Soo-Yun watches them go. She has a piece of bread in her hand that someone passed her before they left and she hasn't taken a bite of it.

"They'll be okay?" she asks.

"For now. The Covenant isn't going to hurt them. They just want the percentage."

"And if someone decides the percentage isn't worth it later?"

I don't answer that. She's 16 and she already knows the answer anyway.

Chen Liling ends up staying. She crosses the gap between the Covenant group and the workshop door without making a thing of it, just steps to the side when Rourke starts walking his 23 north. She ends up standing near Soo-Yun, who offers her the bread. Liling takes half of it.

Bram's upper floor is 8 sleeping spaces on wood boards with a single window facing the street. The ceiling is low enough that Jae-won has to angle his head near the beams. It smells like sawdust and old oil from the workshop below, which is better than the tutorial zone smelled after the first kappa.

Core group in the upper: Eric, Soo-Yun, Jae-won, Mi-rae, Se-ah. Marcus and Liling take 2 of the remaining spots without asking, which I don't stop. Caitlin takes the last one.

8 people and 0 margin. The second floor has Bram's 14 with a mix from the column who found the building on their own or got directed by someone. The other 16 are distributed across the eastern quarter in pairs, based on Se-ah's instruction. Whether they followed it is something we'll find out at first light.

Caitlin sits against the wall near the window and pulls a worn notebook from her jacket. She opens it to a page dense with small handwriting and holds it at an angle where I can see it without her offering it.

"Tomorrow," she says. "The Iron Table meeting Se-ah agreed to is at the second bell in the market district. That's roughly 2 hours after sunrise by Eoran's cycle. You want to know what you're walking into before you go."

"Go on," Se-ah says, from across the room.

"The Iron Table's standing offer to new arrivals has 3 components. First, they'll buy any tutorial loot at 60% of estimated market value, which sounds fair until you realize they set the estimated value. Second, they'll contract labor at a daily rate that covers food but not housing, which keeps contractors dependent. Third, for anyone with a useful class or high stats, they make a membership offer: fixed salary, housing in the market district, and full trade access, in exchange for exclusivity."

"Exclusivity meaning they can't work with anyone else," Marcus says.

"Meaning they belong to the Iron Table," Caitlin says.

The room processes that.

Mi-rae is lying on her boards with one arm over her eyes. She's been quiet since the street. "What's the Red Ledger's rate for outer zone access?"

"10% of anything you bring back. Variable if you're in a group, they take it per head rather than per group."

"So a group of 4 pays 40%," Mi-rae says, arm still over her eyes.

"A group of 4 pays 40%," Caitlin confirms.

The room is quiet for a moment. Outside the window, the street in the eastern quarter has gone low-noise: the kind of evening a working city makes when the day's business is mostly done but the night hasn't properly started.

Se-ah is looking at the ceiling. "The Iron Table meeting is an information exchange. We go, we listen, we don't agree to anything. We find out what they know about the outer zones and the dungeon entries that we can't learn from Caitlin."

"Caitlin is right here," Caitlin says.

"You have been independent and unaffiliated for multiple cycles in a city controlled by 4 factions," Se-ah says, without heat. "What you know is what they let you know."

A beat.

"That's fair," Caitlin says.

The second bell of Eoran's evening rings somewhere in the distance, a low tone that carries across the tiers. Marcus is already asleep against the far wall, out the way people go when they're used to sleeping in shifts and taking the first available window. Soo-Yun is sitting up with her knees to her chest, not sleeping. Liling has her notebook out, writing in it, a small tight script.

I sit near the door with the machete across my knees because it's the only thing I own in Eoran and I'm not ready to put it down yet. The Threat Read passive is quiet. Nothing in the building or the street below reads as hostile attention, which is the best information I have about tonight.

61 arrived. 23 are sleeping in the Covenant's northern tier. 38 are distributed across a city that's been running without them for 6 cycles, in a world the angel described in 4 sentences and then left.

'Tomorrow's going to be a full day.'

I lean my head back against the door frame and don't sleep for a long time.
